1:
"And all the congregation of the children of Israel journeyed from the wilderness of Sin, after their journeys, according to the commandment of the LORD, and pitched in Rephidim: and there was no water for the people to drink." The witnesses note Israel journeying to Rephidim, where there was no water. On "all the congregation... journeyed from the wilderness of Sin... according to the commandment of the LORD," the grammatical voices note the journey. Henry and Gill note that "all the congregation of the children of Israel journeyed from the wilderness of Sin... according to the commandment of the LORD, and pitched in Rephidim" — Israel journeyed by stages (as the LORD directed, by the cloud, Numbers 9:18) and camped at Rephidim; the march under God's direction; the providence guiding the people.

On "according to the commandment of the LORD," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the guidance. Calvin and Ellicott note the journeying "at the commandment of the LORD" (by the pillar, Numbers 9:18) — the people led by God; the providence ordering the march (even to a place of trial).

On "there was no water for the people to drink," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the trial: "there was no water for the people to drink" — the want of water (the trial at Rephidim); the providence (God leading them, by his own command, to a place of need, to try their faith, and to give the water from the rock, vv.6); the test of the wilderness. The verse displays Israel journeying to Rephidim, where there was no water.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the journey — "journeyed from the wilderness of Sin... and pitched in Rephidim: and there was no water," the march (Numbers 9:18, Henry, Gill); "according to the commandment of the LORD," the guidance (Calvin, Ellicott); "there was no water for the people to drink," the trial (vv.6, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying Israel. The verse marks the journey: to Rephidim. The verse displays the guidance: God's command. The verse displays the water: none. The verse displays the trial: of thirst. The journey displays Israel led to the place of trial. The verse displays the Israel: journeying. The verse displays the Rephidim: dry. The verse displays the providence: at work. Israel journeying to Rephidim, where there was no water, displays the trial under God's guidance: they pitched at Rephidim, and there was no water (Numbers 9:18; vv.6) — the people led by God's command to a place of need, the test of faith. The verse displays the people's chiding (v.2).
2:
"Wherefore the people did chide with Moses, and said, Give us water that we may drink. And Moses said unto them, Why chide ye with me? wherefore do ye tempt the LORD?" The witnesses note the people chiding with Moses and tempting the LORD. On "the people did chide with Moses, and said, Give us water that we may drink," the grammatical voices note the chiding. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the people did chide with Moses, and said, Give us water that we may drink" — the people, lacking water (v.1), quarreled (chided, contended) with Moses, demanding water; the murmuring (the recurring unbelief, as at the Red Sea, Exodus 14:11; the bitter waters, 15:24; the manna, 16:2); the contention with the leader.

On "Why chide ye with me?" the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the rebuke. Calvin and Ellicott note Moses' answer ("why chide ye with me?") — the people's quarrel really against God (Moses but his servant); the rebuke of the murmuring; the misdirected complaint.

On "wherefore do ye tempt the LORD?" the grammatical-historical note the sin: "wherefore do ye tempt the LORD?" — the people's chiding was a "tempting" of the LORD (putting God to the test, doubting his presence and care, demanding proof, v.7; Psalm 78:18-20, "they tempted God in their heart"; 95:9, "when your fathers tempted me"); the sin of unbelief (questioning whether the LORD was among them, v.7); the provoking of God. The verse displays the people chiding with Moses and tempting the LORD.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the chiding — "the people did chide with Moses... Why chide ye with me? wherefore do ye tempt the LORD?" the murmuring (Exodus 16:2, Keil, Gill); "Why chide ye with me?" the rebuke (Calvin, Ellicott); "wherefore do ye tempt the LORD?" the sin (Psalm 78:18; 95:9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the chiding people. The verse marks the chiding: with Moses. The verse displays the demand: give us water. The verse displays the rebuke: why chide. The verse displays the tempting: of the LORD. The chiding displays the people's murmuring and unbelief. The verse displays the people: chiding. The verse displays the Moses: rebuking. The verse displays the LORD: tempted. The people chiding with Moses and tempting the LORD display the sin of unbelief: they chided, and Moses said, wherefore do ye tempt the LORD? (Psalm 78:18; 95:9) — the murmuring really against God, the tempting of his presence and care. The verse displays the people's thirst and murmur (v.3).
3:
"And the people thirsted there for water; and the people murmured against Moses, and said, Wherefore is this that thou hast brought us up out of Egypt, to kill us and our children and our cattle with thirst?" The witnesses note the people's thirst and bitter murmuring. On "the people thirsted there for water; and the people murmured against Moses," the grammatical voices note the murmur. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the people thirsted there for water; and the people murmured against Moses" — the people, pressed by thirst, murmured bitterly against Moses; the recurring murmuring (the unbelief of the wilderness); the contention bred of the want.

On "Wherefore is this that thou hast brought us up out of Egypt, to kill us," the reformed and witnesses note the reproach. Ellicott and Gill note the bitter reproach (blaming Moses for bringing them out to die of thirst) — the ingratitude (as at the Red Sea, Exodus 14:11); the murmuring of unbelief (forgetting the late wonders, the manna, the water of Marah).

On "to kill us and our children and our cattle with thirst," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the unbelief: "to kill us and our children and our cattle with thirst" — the exaggerated complaint (charging Moses, and God, with murderous intent); the failing of faith (the people not trusting God to provide, despite the manna); the provoking murmur (that God would yet answer with the water from the rock, vv.6). The verse displays the people's thirst and bitter murmuring.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the murmur — "the people thirsted... and murmured against Moses... to kill us... with thirst," the reproach (Exodus 14:11, Keil, Gill); "Wherefore is this that thou hast brought us up out of Egypt," the ingratitude (Ellicott, Gill); "to kill us and our children and our cattle with thirst," the unbelief (vv.6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the murmuring people. The verse marks the thirst: pressing. The verse displays the murmur: against Moses. The verse displays the reproach: brought us to die. The verse displays the unbelief: shown. The murmur displays the people's failing faith. The verse displays the people: thirsting. The verse displays the Moses: reproached. The verse displays the water: to come. The people's thirst and bitter murmuring display the failing faith: they murmured, wherefore hast thou brought us up to kill us with thirst? (Exodus 14:11) — the ingratitude and unbelief, soon answered by the water from the rock (vv.6). The verse displays Moses' cry to God (v.4).
4:
"And Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, What shall I do unto this people? they be almost ready to stone me." The very brief verse notes Moses crying to the LORD in his distress. The grammatical voices note the cry. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, What shall I do unto this people? they be almost ready to stone me" — Moses, in the crisis (the people's fury, near to stoning him), cried to the LORD for help; the right resort (the leader carrying the trouble to God); the intercessor's prayer; the danger (the people ready to stone him, the violence of the murmuring).

On "What shall I do unto this people?" the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the perplexity. Calvin and Gill note Moses' perplexity (not knowing what to do, the people ungovernable) — the dependence on God; the cry of the troubled servant.

On "they be almost ready to stone me," the grammatical-historical note the danger: "they be almost ready to stone me" — the people's fury (near to violence against Moses); the peril of the leader; the providence (the cry that God would answer with the water from the rock, vv.5-6); the danger driving Moses to God. The verse displays Moses crying to the LORD in his distress.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the cry — "Moses cried unto the LORD... What shall I do... they be almost ready to stone me," the resort to God (Ellicott, Gill); "What shall I do unto this people?" the perplexity (Calvin, Gill); "they be almost ready to stone me," the danger (vv.5-6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the crying Moses. The verse marks the cry: to the LORD. The verse displays the perplexity: what shall I do. The verse displays the danger: stoning. The verse displays the resort: to God. The cry displays Moses carrying the trouble to God. The verse displays the Moses: crying. The verse displays the LORD: appealed to. The verse displays the people: furious. Moses crying to the LORD in his distress displays the right resort: Moses cried, what shall I do? they be ready to stone me (vv.5-6) — the leader carrying the trouble to God, soon answered. The verse displays God's command to smite the rock (vv.5-6).
5:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Go on before the people, and take with thee of the elders of Israel; and thy rod, wherewith thou smotest the river, take in thine hand, and go." The very brief verse notes God's command to Moses to take the rod and the elders. The grammatical voices note the command. Wesley and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Go on before the people, and take with thee of the elders of Israel; and thy rod, wherewith thou smotest the river, take in thine hand, and go" — God answered Moses' cry (v.4): bidding him go before the people with the elders (as witnesses) and the rod (the instrument of the wonders, with which he smote the Nile, Exodus 7:20); the preparation for the miracle; the rod and the elders (the sign and the witnesses).

On "take with thee of the elders of Israel," the witnesses note the witnesses. Gill notes the elders taken (as witnesses of the miracle) — the credible testimony; the elders to see the wonder.

On "thy rod, wherewith thou smotest the river, take in thine hand," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the rod: "thy rod, wherewith thou smotest the river, take in thine hand, and go" — the rod (the instrument of the plagues, Exodus 7:20) now to bring water from the rock; the sign of God's power; the providence (the rod that brought judgment now bringing mercy, the water from the rock, v.6). The verse displays God's command to Moses to take the rod and the elders.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Go on before the people... take with thee of the elders... and thy rod... take in thine hand," the preparation (v.4, Wesley, Gill); "take with thee of the elders of Israel," the witnesses (Gill); "thy rod, wherewith thou smotest the river, take in thine hand," the rod (Exodus 7:20, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanding LORD. The verse marks the command: go before. The verse displays the elders: witnesses. The verse displays the rod: in his hand. The verse displays the miracle: to come. The command displays God preparing the miracle. The verse displays the LORD: commanding. The verse displays the rod: the sign. The verse displays the water: to come. God's command to Moses to take the rod and the elders displays the preparation for the miracle: take the elders and the rod, and go (Exodus 7:20) — the witnesses and the sign for the water from the rock (v.6). The verse displays the rock smitten (v.6).
6:
"Behold, I will stand before thee there upon the rock in Horeb; and thou shalt smite the rock, and there shall come water out of it, that the people may drink. And Moses did so in the sight of the elders of Israel." The witnesses note the rock smitten and the water given — the crux of the smitten Rock, a type of Christ. On "I will stand before thee there upon the rock in Horeb," the grammatical voices note the presence. Gill and Clarke note that "I will stand before thee there upon the rock in Horeb; and thou shalt smite the rock, and there shall come water out of it" — God promised to stand (be present) on the rock at Horeb, and bade Moses smite it with the rod, that water might come out for the people; the divine presence at the rock; the miracle (water from the flinty rock, the gift of life in the desert).

On "thou shalt smite the rock, and there shall come water out of it," interpreters expound the great type. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the type — the rock smitten, water flowing — the witnesses with one voice note the great fulfillment in Christ: "they drank of that spiritual Rock that followed them: and that Rock was Christ" (1 Corinthians 10:4); the smitten rock a type of Christ smitten (the rock struck with the rod, as Christ smitten by the law and the justice of God, Isaiah 53:4-5, "smitten of God, and afflicted"); the water flowing from the smitten rock the type of the living water, the Spirit and grace, flowing from the crucified Christ (John 7:37-39, "out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water"; John 19:34, the blood and water from his pierced side); the rock smitten once (the later rock to be spoken to, not smitten, Numbers 20:8-11, Christ once offered, Hebrews 9:28); the gift of life from the smitten Rock; "God standing on the rock" (the LORD himself in the rock, the deity in Christ); the spiritual drink of the redeemed.

On "that the people may drink. And Moses did so," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the supply: "that the people may drink. And Moses did so in the sight of the elders" — the water given for the murmuring people (the grace abounding to the unworthy, the rock smitten for the rebellious); the supply of life (the abundant water, Psalm 78:15-16, 20; 105:41); the providence (the gift confirmed before the elders, the type of Christ the living water). The verse displays the rock smitten and the water given.

The witnesses agree the substance: God bade Moses smite the rock, and water flowed for the people (Gill, Calvin, Clarke). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the smitten Rock: the rock smitten, the water flowing — a type of Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4, "that Rock was Christ"), the rock smitten as Christ smitten (Isaiah 53:4-5), the living water flowing from the crucified Christ (John 7:37-39; 19:34), the rock smitten once (Hebrews 9:28); the gift of life from the smitten Rock, the spiritual drink of the redeemed (Psalm 78:15-16). The verse displays the smitten-Rock crux. The verse marks the rock: smitten. The verse displays the Christ: the Rock. The rock displays Christ smitten, the living water flowing. The rock smitten and the water given display the crux: smite the rock, and water shall come out (1 Corinthians 10:4; Isaiah 53:4-5; John 7:37-39; 19:34) — the smitten Rock a type of Christ smitten, the living water flowing from the crucified Saviour. The verse displays the place named Massah and Meribah (v.7).
7:
"And he called the name of the place Massah, and Meribah, because of the chiding of the children of Israel, and because they tempted the LORD, saying, Is the LORD among us, or not?" The witnesses note the place named Massah and Meribah for the people's tempting. On "he called the name of the place Massah, and Meribah," the grammatical voices note the names. Ellicott and Gill note that "he called the name of the place Massah (temptation), and Meribah (chiding, strife), because of the chiding of the children of Israel, and because they tempted the LORD" — Moses named the place Massah ("temptation") and Meribah ("strife"), commemorating the people's chiding and tempting of the LORD; the memorial of the sin (the place a lasting witness to the murmuring); the names of the rebellion.

On "because they tempted the LORD," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the sin. Calvin and Gill note the tempting of the LORD (the sin, Psalm 95:8-9, "harden not your heart, as in the provocation, and as in the day of temptation in the wilderness"; quoted, Hebrews 3:8-9) — the provoking of God by unbelief; the sin remembered as a warning (the place a perpetual caution against tempting God).

On "Is the LORD among us, or not?" the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the unbelief: "Is the LORD among us, or not?" — the heart of the tempting (doubting God's presence and care, despite all the wonders); the unbelief (questioning whether God was with them); the sin (the people demanding proof, after all the signs); the warning (Psalm 95; Hebrews 3:7-11, the wilderness generation a warning to all). The verse displays the place named Massah and Meribah.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the names — "he called the name of the place Massah, and Meribah... because they tempted the LORD," the memorial (Ellicott, Gill); "because they tempted the LORD," the sin (Psalm 95:8-9; Hebrews 3:8-9, Calvin, Gill); "Is the LORD among us, or not?" the unbelief (Hebrews 3:7-11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named place. The verse marks the names: Massah, Meribah. The verse displays the chiding: of the people. The verse displays the tempting: of the LORD. The verse displays the unbelief: is the LORD among us. The names display the memorial of the people's sin. The verse displays the place: named. The verse displays the sin: remembered. The verse displays the warning: lasting. The place named Massah and Meribah for the people's tempting displays the memorial of the sin: Massah and Meribah, because they tempted the LORD, Is the LORD among us? (Psalm 95:8-9; Hebrews 3:7-11) — the murmuring and unbelief commemorated, a lasting warning against tempting God. The verse displays the coming of Amalek (v.8).
8:
"Then came Amalek, and fought with Israel in Rephidim." The very brief verse notes Amalek attacking Israel at Rephidim. The grammatical voices note the attack. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "then came Amalek, and fought with Israel in Rephidim" — Amalek (the descendants of Esau, Genesis 36:12; a fierce, predatory desert people) came and fought against Israel at Rephidim; the first armed enemy after the Exodus; the unprovoked attack (falling on the rear, the feeble and weary, Deuteronomy 25:17-18); the foe of God's people.

On "came Amalek, and fought with Israel," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the enmity. Calvin and Wesley note Amalek's attack (the cruel, cowardly assault on the weary people) — the first of Israel's enemies; the type (the fathers note) of the enemies of God's people, the flesh and the powers of darkness warring against the redeemed (Galatians 5:17); the perpetual foe.

On "in Rephidim," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the place: "in Rephidim" — at the place of the water-trial (vv.1-7); the trial of war following the trial of thirst; the providence (the enemy permitted to test and exercise Israel, and to be the occasion of the victory by Moses' uplifted hands, vv.9-13, and the perpetual war, vv.14-16). The verse displays Amalek attacking Israel at Rephidim.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the attack — "then came Amalek, and fought with Israel in Rephidim," the assault (Deuteronomy 25:17, Keil, Henry); "came Amalek, and fought with Israel," the enmity (Galatians 5:17, Calvin, Wesley); "in Rephidim," the place (vv.9-13, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the attacking Amalek. The verse marks the Amalek: came. The verse displays the fight: with Israel. The verse displays the enmity: of the foe. The verse displays the war: begun. The attack displays the enemy of God's people. The verse displays the Amalek: fighting. The verse displays the Israel: assailed. The verse displays the war: to come. Amalek attacking Israel at Rephidim displays the enemy of God's people: Amalek came and fought with Israel (Deuteronomy 25:17-18) — the first foe after the Exodus, a type of the enemies of the redeemed. The verse displays Moses sending Joshua to fight (v.9).
9:
"And Moses said unto Joshua, Choose us out men, and go out, fight with Amalek: to morrow I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God in mine hand." The witnesses note Moses sending Joshua to fight while he holds the rod. On "Moses said unto Joshua, Choose us out men, and go out, fight with Amalek," the grammatical voices note the command. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses said unto Joshua, Choose us out men, and go out, fight with Amalek" — Moses appointed Joshua (here first named, the future leader, Numbers 27:18) to choose men and fight Amalek; the use of means (the battle to be fought, the men armed); Joshua the captain (the warrior, a type, the fathers note, of Jesus, the same name, the captain of salvation, leading God's people against their foes, Hebrews 2:10).

On "go out, fight with Amalek," the reformed and witnesses note the means. Gill notes the use of means (the battle fought by Joshua and the men) joined to the prayer of Moses (v.11) — the lawful means and the prayer together; the war of God's people.

On "I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God in mine hand," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the rod: "I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God in mine hand" — Moses on the hill with the rod (the rod of God, the instrument of the wonders, Exodus 4:20; 7:20; 17:5) — holding it up in prayer and as the sign of God's power (the victory depending not on the sword but on the uplifted rod and hands, vv.11); the intercession (Moses praying on the hill while Joshua fought); the providence (the means and the prayer, the victory of God). The verse displays Moses sending Joshua to fight while he holds the rod.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Choose us out men... fight with Amalek... I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God," the means and prayer (vv.11, Keil, Gill); "go out, fight with Amalek," the means (Gill); "I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God in mine hand," the rod (Exodus 4:20, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanding Moses. The verse marks the Joshua: to fight. The verse displays the means: the battle. The verse displays the rod: of God. The verse displays the prayer: on the hill. The command displays Moses joining means and prayer. The verse displays the Moses: directing. The verse displays the Joshua: the captain. The verse displays the rod: uplifted. Moses sending Joshua to fight while he holds the rod displays the means and the prayer: Joshua fight Amalek, I will stand with the rod of God (vv.11; Hebrews 2:10) — the lawful means joined to the prayer of faith, the victory of God. The verse displays the battle and the uplifted hands (vv.10-11).
10:
"So Joshua did as Moses had said to him, and fought with Amalek: and Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill." The very brief verse notes Joshua fighting while Moses, Aaron, and Hur go up the hill. The grammatical voices note the obedience. Gill and Calvin note that "Joshua did as Moses had said to him, and fought with Amalek: and Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill" — Joshua obeyed, fighting Amalek (the use of means), while Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up the hill (Moses to hold up the rod in prayer, v.11; Aaron and Hur to support him, v.12); the obedience of Joshua; the going-up to pray (the battle below, the prayer above).

On "Joshua did as Moses had said... and fought with Amalek," the witnesses note the means. Gill notes Joshua's obedience (fighting the battle) — the lawful means; the warrior at his task.

On "Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the prayer: "Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill" — Moses going up to pray (with the rod, v.11), Aaron and Hur with him (to support his hands, v.12); the intercession (the battle won by prayer above as much as by the sword below); the providence (the prayer and the means together). The verse displays Joshua fighting while Moses, Aaron, and Hur go up the hill.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Joshua did as Moses had said... and Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill," the means and prayer (v.11, Gill, Calvin); "Joshua did as Moses had said... and fought with Amalek," the means (Gill); "Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill," the prayer (v.12, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fighting Joshua. The verse marks the Joshua: fighting. The verse displays the Moses: going up. The verse displays the Aaron and Hur: with him. The verse displays the prayer: above. The obedience displays the means and the prayer joined. The verse displays the Joshua: obeying. The verse displays the hill: ascended. The verse displays the intercession: begun. Joshua fighting while Moses, Aaron, and Hur go up the hill displays the means and prayer: Joshua fought, Moses went up to pray (v.11-12) — the battle below, the intercession above. The verse displays the uplifted hands (v.11).
11:
"And it came to pass, when Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed: and when he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed." The witnesses note the victory tied to Moses' uplifted hands — the crux of prevailing prayer. On "when Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed," interpreters expound the lifted hands. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the great sign — "when Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed: and when he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed": the battle's tide rose and fell with Moses' hands — when he held them up, Israel prevailed; when he let them down, Amalek prevailed; the witnesses note the lifted hands as a gesture of prayer (the uplifting of hands the posture of prayer and intercession, Psalm 28:2; 63:4; 134:2; 1 Timothy 2:8, "lifting up holy hands"); Moses interceding with uplifted hands (and the rod, v.9) — the victory depending on prayer, not on the sword alone; the lesson — the battles of God's people are won by prayer; the intercession of the man of God prevails (the power of prayer in the conflict, as the church's victory hangs on prayer); when prayer flags, the enemy prevails (the need of persevering, unwearied prayer); the type (the fathers note) of Christ's continual intercession (Hebrews 7:25, ever living to make intercession), and of the believer's dependence on prayer in the spiritual warfare (Ephesians 6:18).

On "when he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the flagging. Wesley and Clarke note that when Moses' hands sank (weary, v.12), Amalek prevailed — the lesson (when prayer flags, the enemy gains; the need of constancy in prayer); the dependence of the victory on the unceasing intercession.

On the sign, the puritan and grammatical-historical note the lesson: the victory tied to the uplifted hands — the power of prayer (the battle God's, won by intercession); the providence (God teaching that the deliverance is by prayer and his power, not by might, Zechariah 4:6); the encouragement to prayer. The verse displays the victory tied to Moses' uplifted hands.

The witnesses agree the substance: when Moses' hands were up, Israel prevailed; when down, Amalek prevailed (Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of prevailing prayer: Moses' uplifted hands a gesture of prayer (Psalm 28:2; 1 Timothy 2:8), the victory depending on intercession, not the sword; the battles of God's people won by prayer, and lost when prayer flags; the need of persevering, supported prayer (v.12); a type of Christ's continual intercession (Hebrews 7:25) and the believer's dependence on prayer in spiritual warfare (Ephesians 6:18). The verse displays the prevailing-prayer crux. The verse marks the hands: held up. The verse displays the victory: Israel prevailed. The verse displays the flagging: Amalek prevailed. The verse displays the prayer: prevailing. The hands display the victory won by prayer. The verse displays the Moses: interceding. The verse displays the battle: by prayer. The verse displays the Christ: foreshadowed. The victory tied to Moses' uplifted hands displays the crux: when Moses held up his hand, Israel prevailed (Psalm 28:2; 1 Timothy 2:8; Hebrews 7:25; Ephesians 6:18) — the battle won by prevailing prayer, the intercession of the man of God, a type of Christ's continual intercession. The verse displays the supporting of Moses' hands (v.12).
12:
"But Moses' hands were heavy; and they took a stone, and put it under him, and he sat thereon; and Aaron and Hur stayed up his hands, the one on the one side, and the other on the other side; and his hands were steady until the going down of the sun." The witnesses note Aaron and Hur staying up Moses' weary hands. On "Moses' hands were heavy; and they took a stone, and put it under him," the grammatical voices note the weariness. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses' hands were heavy; and they took a stone, and put it under him, and he sat thereon; and Aaron and Hur stayed up his hands" — Moses' hands grew weary (heavy) with the long holding-up; so Aaron and Hur set a stone for him to sit, and held up his hands on either side; the support of the intercessor (Moses' weariness, the helpers staying his hands); the lesson (the man of prayer needing support, the weakness of the flesh in the long intercession).

On "Aaron and Hur stayed up his hands, the one on the one side, and the other on the other side," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the help. Calvin and Clarke note Aaron and Hur supporting Moses' hands — the help of brethren in prayer (the supporting of the intercessor); the lesson (the value of mutual help in prayer, the holding-up of one another's hands, Galatians 6:2, bearing one another's burdens; the strengthening of the hands that hang down, Hebrews 12:12).

On "his hands were steady until the going down of the sun," the grammatical-historical note the perseverance: "his hands were steady until the going down of the sun" — the hands held up (steady, firm) till sunset (the prayer sustained to the end); the perseverance in prayer (the victory secured by the unwearied intercession, supported by the helpers); the providence (the prayer maintained, the battle won, v.13). The verse displays Aaron and Hur staying up Moses' weary hands.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the support — "Aaron and Hur stayed up his hands... his hands were steady until the going down of the sun," the help (Galatians 6:2, Ellicott, Gill); "Aaron and Hur stayed up his hands," the mutual help (Hebrews 12:12, Calvin, Clarke); "his hands were steady until the going down of the sun," the perseverance (v.13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the supported Moses. The verse marks the hands: heavy. The verse displays the helpers: Aaron and Hur. The verse displays the support: on each side. The verse displays the steadiness: till sunset. The support displays the intercessor upheld in prayer. The verse displays the Moses: weary. The verse displays the hands: stayed up. The verse displays the prayer: sustained. Aaron and Hur staying up Moses' weary hands displays the support of the intercessor: they stayed up his hands, steady till sunset (Galatians 6:2; Hebrews 12:12) — the help of brethren in prayer, the perseverance that secured the victory. The verse displays the discomfiture of Amalek (v.13).
13:
"And Joshua discomfited Amalek and his people with the edge of the sword." The very brief verse notes Joshua's victory over Amalek. The grammatical voices note the victory. Gill and Wesley note that "Joshua discomfited Amalek and his people with the edge of the sword" — Joshua defeated (discomfited, overthrew) Amalek and his army with the sword; the victory won (by the means, the sword, and the prayer, vv.11-12); the overthrow of the enemy; the deliverance of God's people.

On "discomfited Amalek and his people," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the means. Calvin and Clarke note the victory by the sword (the lawful means) joined to the prayer (Moses' hands, vv.11-12) — the means and the prayer together; the enemy overthrown.

On "with the edge of the sword," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "with the edge of the sword" — the victory by the sword (yet won by prayer, vv.11); the deliverance from the first foe; the providence (the battle the LORD's, won by prayer and means, the type of the spiritual warfare, Ephesians 6:17-18, the sword of the Spirit and prayer). The verse displays Joshua's victory over Amalek.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the victory — "Joshua discomfited Amalek and his people with the edge of the sword," the overthrow (vv.11-12, Gill, Wesley); "discomfited Amalek and his people," the means and prayer (Calvin, Clarke); "with the edge of the sword," the deliverance (Ephesians 6:17, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the victorious Joshua. The verse marks the victory: discomfited Amalek. The verse displays the sword: the means. The verse displays the prayer: the cause. The verse displays the deliverance: won. The victory displays the enemy overthrown by means and prayer. The verse displays the Joshua: conquering. The verse displays the Amalek: defeated. The verse displays the battle: won. Joshua's victory over Amalek displays the deliverance by means and prayer: Joshua discomfited Amalek with the sword (vv.11-12; Ephesians 6:17-18) — the enemy overthrown by the sword and the uplifted hands. The verse displays the memorial commanded (vv.14-16).
14:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Write this for a memorial in a book, and rehearse it in the ears of Joshua: for I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven." The witnesses note the doom of Amalek written for a memorial. On "Write this for a memorial in a book, and rehearse it in the ears of Joshua," the grammatical voices note the record. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Write this for a memorial in a book, and rehearse it in the ears of Joshua" — God commanded Moses to write the matter (the victory and the doom of Amalek) in a book (the first mention of writing Scripture, the inspired record) and rehearse it to Joshua (who would have to do with Amalek); the memorial (the lasting record); the commission (the doom of Amalek committed to Joshua, and the future, Deuteronomy 25:17-19; 1 Samuel 15).

On "Write this for a memorial in a book," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the Scripture. Calvin and Clarke note the writing in a book (the inspired record, the first express command to write Scripture) — the perpetuating of God's word and works; the memorial for the generations.

On "I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the doom: "I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven" — the doom of Amalek (the total destruction decreed, for their unprovoked cruelty against God's people, Deuteronomy 25:17-19; fulfilled in part by Saul, 1 Samuel 15, and David, 1 Samuel 30; the remnant destroyed, 1 Chronicles 4:43); the judgment on the enemy of God's people (the type of the final destruction of all God's enemies); the perpetual war (v.16). The verse displays the doom of Amalek written for a memorial.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the record — "Write this for a memorial in a book... I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek," the memorial and doom (Deuteronomy 25:17, Ellicott, Gill); "Write this for a memorial in a book," the Scripture (Calvin, Clarke); "I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven," the doom (1 Samuel 15, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the recording LORD. The verse marks the record: in a book. The verse displays the Joshua: rehearsed to. The verse displays the doom: of Amalek. The verse displays the judgment: decreed. The record displays the doom of Amalek perpetuated. The verse displays the LORD: commanding. The verse displays the Amalek: doomed. The verse displays the Scripture: written. The doom of Amalek written for a memorial displays the judgment on the enemy: write it; I will put out the remembrance of Amalek (Deuteronomy 25:17-19; 1 Samuel 15) — the doom of the foe of God's people recorded, the type of the final judgment. The verse displays the altar Jehovah-Nissi (v.15).
15:
"And Moses built an altar, and called the name of it Jehovahnissi:" The witnesses note Moses building the altar Jehovah-Nissi — the crux of "the LORD my banner." On "Moses built an altar," the grammatical voices note the altar. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses built an altar, and called the name of it Jehovahnissi" — Moses built an altar (in thanksgiving and memorial of the victory) and named it; the altar of praise (giving God the glory of the victory); the memorial of the deliverance.

On "called the name of it Jehovahnissi," interpreters expound the name. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the name — "Jehovahnissi" ("the LORD is my banner"): Moses named the altar "the LORD my banner" (Hebrew nes, a banner, standard, ensign); the LORD himself Israel's banner (the standard under which they fought and conquered; the rallying-point and the cause of the victory); the witnesses note the lesson — the LORD the banner of his people (the standard of their warfare, the ensign for the nations, Isaiah 11:10, 12; the banner of love, Song of Songs 2:4; Christ lifted up as the banner, John 12:32; the cross the banner of the redeemed); the victory ascribed to the LORD (not to the sword, but to God their banner); the praise of the deliverer (the altar a confession that the LORD won the battle).

On the name, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the praise: "Jehovahnissi" — the praise of God for the victory (the LORD the banner, the giver of the triumph); the providence (the altar a memorial that the LORD fights for his people, the banner of their war); the confession of faith. The verse displays Moses building the altar Jehovah-Nissi.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses built an altar and named it "the LORD my banner" (Keil, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The phrase bears its weight as the crux of "the LORD my banner": "Jehovahnissi" — the LORD himself Israel's banner (the standard of their warfare, the cause of the victory, the rallying-point), the victory ascribed to God not the sword; the LORD the ensign for the nations (Isaiah 11:10), Christ lifted up as the banner (John 12:32), the cross the banner of the redeemed; the altar a confession that the LORD won the battle and is the banner of his people. The verse displays the Jehovah-Nissi crux. The verse marks the altar: built. The verse displays the name: the LORD my banner. The verse displays the banner: the LORD. The verse displays the victory: ascribed to God. The altar displays the LORD the banner of his people. The verse displays the Moses: building. The verse displays the LORD: the banner. The verse displays the praise: ascribed. Moses building the altar Jehovah-Nissi displays the crux: the LORD my banner (Isaiah 11:10, 12; John 12:32) — the LORD himself Israel's banner, the victory ascribed to God, the ensign of the redeemed, Christ lifted up as the banner. The verse displays the perpetual war with Amalek (v.16).
16:
"For he said, Because the LORD hath sworn that the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation." The witnesses note the LORD's perpetual war with Amalek. On "the LORD hath sworn that the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation," the grammatical voices note the oath. Keil and Delitzsch and JFB note that "Because the LORD hath sworn (or, the hand upon the throne of the LORD) that the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation" — the LORD swore (or, lifted his hand to his throne in oath) perpetual war against Amalek from generation to generation; the divine enmity (God himself at war with Amalek, the cruel enemy of his people, Deuteronomy 25:17-19); the perpetual judgment (the war until Amalek's destruction, 1 Samuel 15).

On "the LORD hath sworn," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the oath. Calvin and Gill note the LORD's oath (the solemn, irrevocable decree of war against Amalek) — the divine enmity sworn; the certainty of the judgment.

On "war with Amalek from generation to generation," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the perpetuity: "war with Amalek from generation to generation" — the perpetual war (until Amalek's utter destruction, fulfilled through the generations, Saul, David, Hezekiah's days, 1 Chronicles 4:43); the judgment on the enemy of God's people (the type of God's perpetual war against the enemies of his church, the powers of darkness, until their final overthrow, Revelation 19:11-21); the certainty of the doom. The verse displays the LORD's perpetual war with Amalek.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the oath — "the LORD hath sworn that the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation," the perpetual war (Deuteronomy 25:17, Keil, JFB); "the LORD hath sworn," the oath (Calvin, Gill); "war with Amalek from generation to generation," the perpetuity (1 Samuel 15, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warring LORD. The verse marks the oath: sworn. The verse displays the war: with Amalek. The verse displays the generations: perpetual. The verse displays the doom: certain. The oath displays God's perpetual enmity with the foe. The verse displays the LORD: at war. The verse displays the Amalek: doomed. The verse displays the judgment: perpetual. The LORD's perpetual war with Amalek displays the divine enmity with the foe: the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation (Deuteronomy 25:17-19; 1 Samuel 15) — God himself at war with the enemy of his people, a type of his war against the enemies of his church. The verse closes the chapter, before Jethro's visit (ch.18).
